0.title: Each producer runs a local agent that batches records on disk before forwarding them upstream.
0.detail: Each producer runs a thin local agent that batches records on disk before forwarding them upstream. Disk-buffering is the linchpin of the design — it decouples producer liveness from upstream availability and absorbs transient outages without dropping records. The agent flushes batches when either the time-since-last-flush exceeds five seconds or the buffer reaches one megabyte, whichever fires first.

1.title: Records are serialized as length-prefixed JSON to simplify recovery from partial writes on agent restart.
1.detail: Records are serialized as length-prefixed JSON. The length prefix simplifies recovery from partial writes: on agent restart, the tail of the buffer file is scanned for a complete record boundary, and any trailing partial record is discarded. We accept the small replay risk this implies in exchange for a recovery procedure that fits in a single function.

2.title: Buffer files are rotated when they reach one hundred megabytes or twelve hours of age, and renamed files are uploaded in order.
2.detail: Local rotation policy is straightforward. Once a buffer file reaches one hundred megabytes or twelve hours of age, it is renamed and a new file is opened. Renamed files are eligible for upload; the upload coordinator chooses the oldest unsent file first, ensuring that backlogged records drain in order.

3.title: Producers do not perform schema validation; the agent's job is to move bytes durably, leaving schema concerns to the central pipeline.
3.detail: Producers do not perform schema validation. The agent's job is to move bytes durably; schema concerns belong to the central pipeline, where they can be evolved without redeploying the entire fleet.

source:
# Producer side

Each producer runs a thin local agent that batches records on disk before forwarding them upstream. Disk-buffering is the linchpin of the design — it decouples producer liveness from upstream availability and absorbs transient outages without dropping records. The agent flushes batches when either the time-since-last-flush exceeds five seconds or the buffer reaches one megabyte, whichever fires first.

Records are serialized as length-prefixed JSON. The length prefix simplifies recovery from partial writes: on agent restart, the tail of the buffer file is scanned for a complete record boundary, and any trailing partial record is discarded. We accept the small replay risk this implies in exchange for a recovery procedure that fits in a single function.

Local rotation policy is straightforward. Once a buffer file reaches one hundred megabytes or twelve hours of age, it is renamed and a new file is opened. Renamed files are eligible for upload; the upload coordinator chooses the oldest unsent file first, ensuring that backlogged records drain in order.

Producers do not perform schema validation. The agent's job is to move bytes durably; schema concerns belong to the central pipeline, where they can be evolved without redeploying the entire fleet.
